docs.title: Welcome to MkDocs
docs.text: For full documentation visit mkdocs.org.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
• mkdocs new [dir-name] - Create a new project.
  • mkdocs serve - Start the live-reloading docs server.
  • mkdocs build - Build the documentation site.
  • mkdocs -h - Print help message and exit.

docs.title: Project layout
docs.text:
mkdocs.yml    # The configuration file.
docs/
    index.md  # The documentation homepage.
    ...       # Other markdown pages, images and other files.

docs.title: Course Description for Engineering Practicum IV Honors
Course Description: Engineering Practicum IV Honors
Course Overview
docs.text:
Engineering Practicum IV Honors enables students to examine technology and engineering fundamentals related to solving real-world problems. Students examine ethics and intellectual property and design a practicum project — a culmination of knowledge and skill gained in previous engineering courses. In addition, students continue to investigate engineering specialty fields and related careers to determine whether they are good candidates for postsecondary educational opportunities in engineering.

This course serves as the capstone experience of the high school engineering sequence, integrating technical, professional, and interpersonal skills developed throughout prior coursework. Students work independently and collaboratively to propose, design, execute, document, and present an original engineering solution to a real-world challenge.

docs.title: Target Audience
docs.text: High school students (Honors level)

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Engineering II with instructor permission, or Engineering III

docs.title: Main Topics Covered
docs.text:
1. Personal and Professional Qualities — creativity, critical thinking, initiative, integrity, and work ethic
  2. Interpersonal and Team Skills — conflict resolution, communication, diversity, customer service, and collaboration
  3. Professional Competencies — career management, information literacy, information security, current IT systems, mathematical applications, workplace safety, and professionalism
  4. Industry Examination — planning, management, financial responsibility, technical production, technology principles, labor issues, community issues, and health/safety/environmental concerns
  5. Student Organization and Civic Engagement — purposes of student organizations, leadership, membership responsibilities, and internet safety
  6. Work-Based Learning (WBL) — types of WBL, career exploration, and reflective practice
  7. Employability Skills — career research, educational pathways, portfolio development, internship/job application processes, engineering design vs. scientific method
  8. Intellectual Property and Ethics — engineering ethics, intellectual property rights, and professional vs. personal ethics
  9. Practicum Project Design and Execution — systems thinking, engineering design process, proposal development, peer review, project management, scheduling, budgeting, cost estimation, modeling and simulation, risk analysis, and stakeholder communication
  10. Career Preparation — postsecondary research, certifications and licensure, mock interviews, and technical life skills

docs.title: Topics NOT Covered
docs.text:
• Advanced college-level engineering mathematics (calculus, differential equations)
  • Discipline-specific engineering coursework (e.g., civil, electrical, mechanical in depth)
  • Manufacturing or fabrication at an industrial scale
  • Post-secondary coursework content (university-level engineering theory)

docs.title: Learning Outcomes
docs.text: After this course, students will be able to:

docs.title: Remember
docs.text:
Retrieve, recognize, and recall relevant knowledge

  • Identify types of intellectual property rights and how they are protected and enforced
  • Identify the need or opportunity for an engineering solution
  • Identify educational requirements for a chosen engineering field
  • Identify the process of applying for an engineering internship or job
  • Identify the purposes and goals of a student organization
  • Identify the types of work-based learning (WBL) opportunities available
  • Identify Internet safety issues and procedures for complying with acceptable use standards
  • Research postsecondary education opportunities related to engineering
  • Research cost-estimation tools and methods used in the engineering field
  • Maintain a working knowledge of current information-technology (IT) systems

docs.title: Understand
docs.text:
Construct meaning from instructional messages

  • Explain how ethical behavior of engineers is essential to the betterment of society
  • Explain the benefits and responsibilities of membership in student and professional/civic organizations
  • Demonstrate an understanding of information security
  • Demonstrate big-picture thinking within an engineering or organizational context
  • Examine principles of technology that underlie an industry or organization
  • Examine aspects of planning within an industry or organization
  • Examine aspects of financial responsibility within an industry or organization
  • Examine labor issues related to an industry or organization
  • Examine community issues related to an industry or organization
  • Examine health, safety, and environmental issues related to an industry or organization

docs.title: Apply
docs.text:
Carry out or use procedures in given situations

  • Apply mathematical skills to job-specific engineering tasks
  • Apply model and simulation techniques to create a working model
  • Use the engineering design process to complete the practicum project
  • Use engineering project management tools throughout the practicum
  • Develop a schedule using industry-standard applications to ensure project completion
  • Develop a budget for the practicum project
  • Create a work schedule aligned with project milestones
  • Demonstrate proficiency with technologies, tools, and machines relevant to the chosen engineering field
  • Demonstrate workplace safety practices appropriate to the engineering environment
  • Participate in a work-based learning experience when appropriate

docs.title: Analyze
docs.text:
Break material into parts and determine relationships

  • Analyze the similarities and differences between the engineering design process and the scientific method
  • Analyze the potential risk list for the practicum project
  • Examine technical and production skills required of workers within an industry
  • Examine aspects of management within an industry or organization
  • Compare requirements for select professional engineering certifications and licensure
  • Compare professional and personal ethics in the context of engineering practice
  • Demonstrate information-literacy skills by critically evaluating engineering and career resources
  • Reflect on lessons learned during the work-based learning experience
  • Explore career opportunities related to the WBL experience
  • Research a principal field of interest in engineering and its scope

docs.title: Evaluate
docs.text:
Make judgments based on criteria and standards

  • Evaluate the safety of engineering designs against established standards
  • Peer review all project proposals using structured criteria
  • Demonstrate critical thinking and problem solving in evaluating engineering challenges
  • Demonstrate conflict-resolution skills when navigating team disagreements
  • Demonstrate customer service skills in communicating with project stakeholders
  • Demonstrate career- and life-management skills by assessing personal strengths against engineering pathways
  • Demonstrate continuous learning and adaptability when faced with project setbacks
  • Manage time and resources effectively throughout the practicum timeline
  • Demonstrate respect for diversity in team and professional settings
  • Demonstrate integrity and professionalism in all project and workplace interactions

docs.title: Create
docs.text:
Put elements together to form coherent wholes; includes capstone projects

  • Design and complete an original engineering practicum project using the full engineering design process
  • Use systems thinking to develop a comprehensive engineering solution
  • Present a proposal to an engineering challenge, incorporating research, design, and feasibility analysis
  • Document the full progression of the engineering practicum in a formal technical report
  • Communicate the final solution to stakeholders through a professional presentation
  • Build a complete work portfolio demonstrating skills and accomplishments across the engineering sequence
  • Demonstrate leadership skills through participation in student organization activities such as meetings, programs, and projects
  • Demonstrate creativity and innovation in developing novel engineering solutions
  • Demonstrate initiative and self-direction throughout the independent practicum project
  • Exhibit technical and life skills necessary for success in the engineering field

docs.title: Chapters
docs.text: This textbook is organized into 8 chapters covering 85 concepts, aligned to the assignments in Engineering Practicum IV Honors.

docs.title: Chapter Overview
docs.text:
1. Engineering Field Exploration - Research engineering fields, educational pathways, and professional credentials.
  2. Engineering Foundations and Industry Examination - Explore how engineers think and how engineering organizations operate.
  3. Portfolio and Employment Preparation - Build the documents and skills needed to pursue engineering opportunities.
  4. Problem Statement, Planning, Ethics, and IP - Define your engineering challenge and understand the ethical and legal responsibilities that guide your work.
  5. Risk Management - Evaluate design safety and analyze risks before committing to a solution.
  6. Project Work Schedule and Budget - Plan the timeline and finances needed to complete your practicum project.
  7. Project Proposal Pitch - Write, present, and peer review your engineering project proposal.
  8. Project Execution, Checkpoints, and Final Presentation - Build, document, and present your completed engineering solution.

docs.title: How to Use This Textbook
docs.text:
Each chapter aligns to one or more assignments in the course. Complete chapters in order — concepts build on each other, and later chapters assume knowledge from earlier ones. Each chapter includes a full list of concepts from the learning graph so you can track your progress.

Note: Each chapter index includes a "TODO: Generate Chapter Content" placeholder. Full content will be added chapter by chapter.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter launches the course by helping students identify and research an engineering field that interests them. Students explore engineering specializations, investigate educational and licensing pathways, and compare certification and degree requirements — building the foundation for every career decision that follows in the course.

Students apply these ideas directly through the Engineering Industry Scavenger Hunt and a structured comparison activity.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter prepares students to enter the engineering job market by building the core documents and skills employers look for — a professional resume, a targeted cover letter, and a complete work portfolio. It culminates in a mock interview experience that puts all of these materials to use in a realistic professional setting.

docs.title: Chapter 4: Problem Statement, Planning, Ethics, and IP
Summary
docs.text: This is the launch chapter for the practicum project. Students identify a real engineering need or opportunity, define their challenge, and begin applying project management thinking to their solution. Woven throughout is an introduction to the ethical and legal responsibilities engineers carry — including professional ethics, intellectual property rights, and the obligations engineers have to the public — framed as essential context for making good design decisions.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Before committing to a design direction, engineers must evaluate whether their solution is safe and identify what could go wrong. This chapter teaches students to apply safety standards to their designs, systematically identify risks, analyze their likelihood and impact, and develop strategies to reduce or eliminate them — a critical step before the project proposal is written.

docs.text: With a problem defined and risks assessed, students now plan the execution of their practicum project in detail. This chapter covers the full project planning toolkit — breaking work into manageable pieces, building a realistic schedule with industry-standard tools, and developing a budget grounded in real cost research — so students can demonstrate to stakeholders that their solution is achievable within the course timeframe.

docs.title: Learning Graph
docs.text: Open Learning Graph Viewer Fullscreen

docs.title: Engineering Practicum IV Honors
docs.text:
This learning graph is the foundational data structure for the Engineering Practicum IV Honors intelligent textbook. It maps 85 key concepts across three competency areas — Employability Skills, Intellectual Property, and Practicum Project Design — and defines the prerequisite relationships between them.

The learning graph supports multiple learning pathways and helps students understand how concepts build upon one another throughout the course.

docs.title: What is a Learning Graph?
docs.text:
A learning graph is a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) in which:

  • Each node represents a concept students must learn
  • Each arrow represents a learning dependency — the concept at the tail should be learned before the concept at the head

docs.title: Concept Categories
docs.text:
The 85 concepts are organized into 11 categories:

  • Foundation Concepts — Engineering design process, scientific method, and their relationship
  • Career Exploration — Engineering fields, specializations, and postsecondary pathways
  • Credentials and Licensing — Degrees, certifications, PE license, and EIT exam
  • Employment Preparation — Portfolios, resumes, applications, and interviews
  • Engineering Ethics — Professional codes, responsibility, and ethical decision making
  • Intellectual Property — Patents, copyrights, trademarks, trade secrets, and enforcement
  • Design Process — Problem identification through prototype development and optimization
  • Systems Thinking — Inputs, outputs, feedback loops, and subsystem interactions
  • Proposals and Review — Writing, structuring, defending, and peer reviewing proposals
  • Project Management — Scheduling, budgeting, risk analysis, and project tools
  • Communication and Documentation — Reports, modeling, stakeholder communication, and presentations

docs.title: Concept Taxonomy: Engineering Practicum IV Honors
docs.text: 11 categories covering all 85 concepts across the three competency areas.

docs.title: 1. Foundation Concepts (FOUND)
docs.text:
Core principles that underpin the rest of the course — the engineering design process, the scientific method, and how they relate to each other.

Concepts: Engineering Design Process, Scientific Method, Hypothesis Testing, Engineering vs Science

docs.title: 2. Career Exploration (CAREER)
docs.text:
Researching engineering fields, specializations, educational pathways, and the credentials that lead to engineering careers.

Concepts: Engineering Career Research, Engineering Specializations, Field of Interest Selection, Postsecondary Education Options, Engineering Programs, College Admissions Process, Degree Requirements, Professional Certifications, Licensure Requirements, Professional Engineer (PE) License, Engineer in Training (EIT) Exam

docs.title: 4. Employment Preparation (EMPLOY)
docs.text:
Practical tools and skills for entering the engineering workforce — portfolios, resumes, applications, and interviews.

Concepts: Work Portfolio, Portfolio Components, Resume Writing, Cover Letter Writing, Job Application Process, Engineering Internships, Interview Skills, Mock Interview Practice, Professional Readiness

docs.title: 5. Engineering Ethics (ETHICS)
docs.text:
The ethical frameworks and responsibilities engineers hold toward society, clients, and the profession.

Concepts: Engineering Ethics, Code of Ethics, Professional Responsibility, Public Safety in Engineering, Ethical Decision Making, Personal vs Professional Ethics, Conflict of Interest

docs.title: 6. Intellectual Property (IP)
docs.text:
Types of intellectual property protections, how they are enforced, and the consequences of infringement.

Concepts: Intellectual Property, Patents, Copyrights, Trademarks, Trade Secrets, IP Protection and Enforcement, IP Infringement

docs.title: 7. Design Process (DESIGN)
docs.text:
The iterative steps of engineering design from problem identification through prototype development and optimization.

Concepts: Problem Identification, Needs Assessment, Opportunity Recognition, Constraints and Criteria, Brainstorming Solutions, Prototype Development, Testing and Iteration, Design Optimization, Design Safety Evaluation, Safety Standards

docs.title: 8. Systems Thinking (SYST)
docs.text:
Understanding engineering problems as interconnected systems with inputs, outputs, feedback loops, and subsystems.

Concepts: Systems Thinking, System Inputs and Outputs, Feedback Loops, Subsystem Interactions

docs.title: 9. Proposals and Review (PROP)
docs.text:
Writing, structuring, defending, and peer-reviewing engineering project proposals.

Concepts: Proposal Writing, Project Proposal Structure, Engineering Challenge Definition, Proposal Defense, Peer Review Process, Proposal Review Criteria, Constructive Feedback

docs.title: 10. Project Management (PROJMGT)
docs.text:
Planning, scheduling, budgeting, and managing risk throughout the engineering practicum project.

Concepts: Project Management, Project Management Software, Work Breakdown Structure, Schedule Development, Scheduling Tools, Gantt Chart, Milestone Planning, Budget Research Methods, Cost Estimation, Cost-Benefit Analysis, Budget Development, Resource Allocation, Work Schedule Creation, Risk Identification, Risk Analysis, Risk Mitigation Strategies

docs.title: 11. Communication and Documentation (COMM)
docs.text:
Documenting project progress, modeling solutions, and communicating results to stakeholders and the public.

Concepts: Technical Report Writing, Documentation Standards, Report Structure, Modeling Techniques, Simulation Software, Stakeholder Identification, Stakeholder Communication, Presentation Skills, Visual Communication, Final Project Presentation

docs.title: Course Description Assessment Report
docs.text: Course: Engineering Practicum IV Honors Assessment Date: 2026-06-18 Assessed File: /docs/course-description.md

docs.title: Overall Score: 100/100
docs.text: Quality Rating: Excellent — Ready for learning graph generation

docs.title: Detailed Scoring Breakdown
docs.text: Element Points Available Points Earned Title 5 5 Target Audience 5 5 Prerequisites 5 5 Main Topics Covered 10 10 Topics Excluded 5 5 Learning Outcomes Header 5 5 Remember Level 10 10 Understand Level 10 10 Apply Level 10 10 Analyze Level 10 10 Evaluate Level 10 10 Create Level 10 10 Descriptive Context 5 5 Total 100 100

docs.title: Scoring Justification
docs.text:
• Title (5/5): "Engineering Practicum IV Honors" is clear, specific, and conveys course level and sequence position.
  • Target Audience (5/5): "High school students (Honors level)" is specific and appropriately qualified.
  • Prerequisites (5/5): Prerequisites are clearly stated — Engineering II with instructor permission, or Engineering III.
  • Main Topics Covered (10/10): Ten well-defined topic areas covering personal qualities, team skills, professional competencies, industry examination, civic engagement, WBL, employability, ethics/IP, practicum design, and career preparation.
  • Topics Excluded (5/5): Four clear exclusions set appropriate boundaries distinguishing high school from post-secondary engineering content.
  • Learning Outcomes Header (5/5): Correctly uses "After this course, students will be able to:" framing.
  • Remember (10/10): Ten specific, verb-driven outcomes using "identify," "research," and "maintain."
  • Understand (10/10): Ten outcomes using "explain," "demonstrate," and "examine" to show conceptual comprehension.
  • Apply (10/10): Ten outcomes using "apply," "use," "develop," "create," and "participate" tied to concrete practicum tasks.
  • Analyze (10/10): Ten outcomes using "analyze," "compare," "examine," "reflect," and "explore" to break down complex relationships.
  • Evaluate (10/10): Ten outcomes using "evaluate," "peer review," "demonstrate," and "manage" with clear judgment criteria.
  • Create (10/10): Ten outcomes including the capstone practicum project, portfolio, technical report, and stakeholder presentation — strong synthesis across the sequence.
  • Descriptive Context (5/5): Course overview effectively explains purpose, relevance, and the integrative capstone role of the course.

docs.title: Gap Analysis
docs.text:
No significant gaps identified. All thirteen elements are present and meet or exceed quality thresholds.

Minor observations (not point-deductions): - The "Topics NOT Covered" section could optionally be expanded with more discipline-specific exclusions (e.g., fluid dynamics, thermodynamics, structural analysis) for even clearer boundaries during concept generation. - Some Bloom's Taxonomy outcomes overlap across levels (e.g., "research" appears in both Remember and Analyze) — this is acceptable and reflects the course's integrated nature, but the learning graph generator should be aware of this when clustering concepts.

docs.title: Concept Generation Readiness
docs.text:
Estimated concept potential: 200+ concepts ✓

Justification: - 10 topic areas × ~10 sub-concepts each = ~100 foundational concepts - 60 learning outcomes across 6 Bloom's levels generate additional procedural, conceptual, and metacognitive concepts - Engineering design process, project management, ethics, IP, WBL, and career pathways each contain dense concept clusters - The capstone practicum alone (systems thinking, risk analysis, scheduling, budgeting, modeling, stakeholder communication) yields 20–30 distinct concepts

The course description is well-suited to support a full 200-concept learning graph.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Score ≥ 85 — Ready to proceed with learning graph generation.

Recommended next skill: learning-graph-generator

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• No category exceeds 30% of total concepts — distribution is healthy.
  • Project Management (PROJMGT) is the largest category at 18.8%, reflecting the depth of the practicum project execution competencies (CTE-55 through CTE-64).
  • Foundation Concepts (FOUND) and Systems Thinking (SYST) are intentionally small — they represent core prerequisite knowledge rather than a standalone topic area.

docs.text: This interactive viewer allows you to explore the learning graph for this course.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Search: Type in the search box to find specific concepts
  • Category Filtering: Use checkboxes to show/hide concept categories
  • Interactive Navigation: Click and drag to explore, scroll to zoom
  • Statistics: View real-time counts of visible nodes and edges

docs.title: Using the Viewer
docs.text:
1. Search for Concepts: Start typing in the search box to find concepts. Click on a result to focus on that node.

  2. Filter by Category: Use the category checkboxes in the sidebar to show or hide groups of related concepts. Use "Check All" or "Uncheck All" for bulk operations.

  3. Navigate the Graph:

  4. Drag to pan around the graph
  5. Scroll to zoom in and out

  6. Click on a node to select it and highlight its connections

  7. View Statistics: The sidebar shows counts of visible nodes, edges, and foundational concepts.

docs.title: Graph Structure
docs.text:
• Foundational Concepts (left side): Prerequisites with no dependencies
  • Advanced Concepts (right side): Topics that build on multiple prerequisites
  • Edges: Arrows point from a concept to its prerequisites

docs.title: Launch the Viewer
docs.text: Open Learning Graph Viewer
